  • Service issues (e.g., inactive or not found)

In the example below, you can see that authentication has failed due to an expired certificate.

SAP Cloud Connector - expired certificate

 If an error does not show up as expected, you can consider increasing the trace level of the logs as shown below. For SSL issues, a TLS trace has to be active, and for other issues, you need to look at the tunnel trace.

SAP Cloud Connector - trace levels

You should also turn on SAP Cloud Connector Alerting so you can be kept informed about critical system issues (expiring certificates, disk space, CPU load etc…) BEFORE THEY OCCUR.

THIS IS ESPECIALLY IMPORTANT IN YOUR PRODUCTION LANDSCAPE!!!!!

EXAMPLE: By turning on Alerts for expiring certificates, an email alert would be sent 30 days before a certificate expires, so a new certificate can be generated and used, thereby preventing any connectivity issues:

Refer to SAP BTP Connectivity - Alerting

 

4. Check your content exposure

You have already run transaction code /UI2/CDM3_EXP_SCOPE to expose your chosen roles to SAP BTP. Refer to:

  • SAP Help: Manage Launchpad Content for Exposure

  • Blog: Joule for SAP S/4HANA Cloud Private Edition - A Comprehensive Setup Guide

Now, you can check if your content has been successfully exposed and is ready for consumption by SAP Business Technology Platform (SAP BTP).

Exposure is available in two versions: the current Version 1 and the new Version 2 as follows:

  • Version 1 is based on legacy target mappings

  • Version 2 provides a seamless end-to-end app model, extending from the SAP Fiori Apps Reference Library to business user usage. Version 2 is based on Launchpad App Descriptor Items (LADIs) and is the long-term, future-proof successor to Version 1.

NOTE: Since apps have different IDs in version 2, there is no compatible upgrade path from version 1 to version 2. Implementing integration using version 2 requires initiating a new project.

Depending on whether you are using Version 1 or Version 2, the following URLs can be used to test your content exposure to SAP BTP:

  • Version 1 http://<your-server-name>:<your-port-number>/sap/bc/ui2/cdm3/entities

  • Version 2 http://<your-server-name>:<your-port-number>/sap/bc/http/ui2/flp_content_exposure/entities

Version 1 and 2 have different URLs, 1 is based on classical tiles and target mappings and 2 is based on LADIs.

The URLs must be run in the browser (not via SAP BTP) and expose content in JSON format. Below, you can see that SAP Fiori App F0233A using semantic object ‘OutboundDelivery’ and action ‘displayFactSheet’ has successfully been exposed from a backend SAP S/4HANA Cloud Private Edition system and can  be read by SAP BTP.

Test the url directly in a  browser (not via SAP BTP) with the system user configured in the design time destination (JOULE_ADMIN in the configuration guide).

Testing content exposureRefer to:

  • SAP Note: 3501486 - Launchpad Content Exposed with version 2 not updated in SAP Build Workzone, Standard edition

  • SAP Note: 3345119 - Expose Launchpad Content to SAP BTP - Exposure Version 2

  • SAP Help: Manage Launchpad Content for Exposure

 

5. SAP Build Work Zone, standard edition

Now you can check your Content Channel, and the associated destinations work correctly

  • Check that the Design Time and Runtime destinations (‘jouledt’ and ‘joulert’) work successfully:Destination TestsExample: An incorrect Design Time destination will cause errors importing roles from SAP S/4HANA Cloud  Private Edition. NOTE: The Ping does *not* fully check the entire destination. If it is “red” you know something is wrong, but “green” does not mean that everything is fine

  • You can check if your Content Channel synchronization works successfully (NOTE: the synchronization will not work if  the destination pings in (a) have not been successfully executed). Click on the ‘Synch’ button to consume content exposed from SAP S/4HANA Cloud Private Edition:

  • Click on the ‘Report’ link to check that there are no synch errors:

  •  Check the Content Explorer to ensure that content exposed through a channel has been successfully consumed by SAP BTP- From the Site Manager page, select Content Manager and then Content Explorer:

  • Chose the Channel through which your content was consumed by SAP Build Work Zone, standard edition:

  • Here you can see the content consumed through your selected channel. In this example, you can see that 31 roles have been exposed from SAP S/4HANA Cloud Private Edition and consumed by SAP BTP.   

  • Check roles exposed from SAP S/4HANA Cloud Private Edition have been successfully consumed by SAP BTP:

  • Check that your test user has the correctly exposed roles assigned:

        NOTE: If you do not see any roles here, check the following:

  • The test user has the necessary roles in SAP S/4HANA Cloud Private Edition

  • Roles have been exposed via Content Exposure

  • SAP Identity Provisioning service jobs have run and completed successfully

Refer to:

  • SAP Build Work Zone, standard edition, standard edition - troubleshooting

  • Integration Guide: SAP Build Work Zone, standard edition, Standard Edition Integration with SAP S/4HANA

 

6. Identity Provisioning service

The navigation service of SAP Build Work Zone, standard edition uses the SAP Identity Provisioning service (IPS). IPS provisions identities and their authorizations between source and target systems. To use the navigational capabilities in Joule, you need to configure SAP S/4HANA Cloud Private Edition as the source and SAP Build Work Zone, standard edition, standard edition as the target system in IPS. Here, you confirm successful provisioning by reviewing logs and status of synchronizing jobs.

- In SAP Cloud Identity Services, view Identity Provisioning -> Provisioning Logs:

Provisioning logs

- Now you can see the Job Execution Logs and check them to ensure there are no errors:

Execution logs

 - In this example, you can see that user BPINST cannot be provisioned correctly by SAP IPS as it has NO email address -> this  user will experience issues with SAP Build Work Zone, standard edition and Joule.

Example execution log

7.    Joule Welcome

Once all the above tests have been successfully executed, you can run Joule. If Joule has been configured correctly then there will be a welcome screen with Joule skills showing as in the example below:

Joule debugger console

Click on the debug icon shown above to check there are no errors in the debugger console:

Now you can go ahead and start using Joule and leverage its business value!

 

8. Help! I have tried all the above checks and still have problems

Don’t worry! There are lots of great troubleshooting tools.

a)  Check if the related Fiori apps on the SAP S/4HANA Fiori Launchpad are working without issues:

      App Support: Troubleshoot configuration and authorization errors for apps in the SAP Fiori launchpad.

     Refer to:

  • App Support
  • Setting Up App Support

       App Inspector: Troubleshoot configuration and authorization errors for apps available through the SAP Fiori Launchpad

  Refer to:

  • App Inspector
  • Setting Up App Inspector

b) In Joule, you have the Debug button (click on the ‘Debug’ button show below):

  • Highlights service issues and general errors
  • Active conversation with Joule
  • Response Payload: Shows aggregated JSON response for each Joule interaction
  • Requests Logs: Shows requests and responses grouped by prompt

Joule debug console

 c)  SAP Cloud Connector Logs. These logs can have the following use cases:

  • Principal propagation and authentication troubleshooting
  • Backend server connectivity (e.g., incorrect hostname or protocol, expired certificates)
  • Service issues (e.g., inactive or not found)

 The logs can be accessed in the SAP Cloud Connector as shown:

SAP Cloud Connector logs

Refer to SAP Help: Cloud Connector Trace and Logging

d) SAP Gateway Error Log

  • Transaction /IWFND/ERROR_LOG
  • Shows service errors (e.g., forbidden, not found, inconsistent calls)

      SAP Gateway Tracing Tools

  • Transaction /IWFND/TRACES
  • Troubleshoot service issues, capture header and payload data

      Internet Communication Manager Monitor

  • Transaction SMICM
  • Detailed HTTP calls (by increasing trace level to level 3)
  • Helps to identify authentication and SSL issues

       Refer to SAP Help: Gateway Tracing Tools

Still stuck? Refer to SAP Help: Joule - Monitoring and Troubleshooting which includes details on raising SAP incidents.

Troubleshooting:

  • Blog: Common configuration issues: Joule for SAP S/4HANA Cloud Private Edition
  • SAP Help: SAP Build Work Zone, standard edition, standard edition - Troubleshooting
  • SAP Help: How to troubleshoot Cloud Connector principal propagation over HTTPS
  • SAP Help: Joule Constraints
  • SAP Note: 3649670 - Missing Joule icon in SAP Build Work Zone, standard edition Standard Edition
  • SAP Note: 3666014 - Information required for Joule
  • SAP Note: 3673511 - Troubleshooting for Joule page display issues
  • SAP Note: 3679363 - How to recognize a Joule response's

Becoming an SAP Fiori for SAP S/4HANA guru

You’ll find much more on the community topic page for SAP Fiori for SAP S/4HANA

Other helpful links in the SAP Community:

  • Follow our tag "S4HANA RIG" in "Enterprise Resource Planning Blog Posts by SAP" - SAP Community 
  • for more from the SAP S/4HANA Customer Care and RIG
  • See all questions and answers about SAP Fiori for SAP S/4HANA
  • Follow SAP Fiori for SAP S/4HANA for more blogs and updates
  • Ask a Question about SAP Fiori for SAP S/4HANA

entry.title: Document Grounding Setup in Joule: Building Smarter Joule Agents with AI Core and S3
entry.content:
Overview:

This guide provides comprehensive instructions for setting up and configuring document grounding capabilities in Joule Studio. Document grounding enables your Joule agent to search through documents, retrieve relevant information, and provide contextually accurate responses based on your organization's specific content.

By following this guide, you will:

  • Set up the required SAP BTP infrastructure (Object Store and SAP AI Core)
  • Create and configure a document grounding resource group
  • Build a Joule agent with document grounding capabilities
  • Apply best practices for prompting agents that use document grounding

Prerequisite:

Before you begin, ensure you have the following:

Requirement

Description

URL's

SAP BTP Global Account

Active global account with administrator access

SAP BTP Global Account

SAP BTP Subaccount

Dedicated subaccount for Joule Studio and AI services

SAP BTP Subaccount

AI Core Entitlement

'aicore` service entitlement assigned to your subaccount

SAP AI Core

Object Store Service

S3-compatible object store service access

Object Store on AWS

SAP Build Process Automation

Joule Studio Capability and Grounding services

SAP Build Process Automation 

Technical Knowledge:

  • Basic understanding of SAP BTP destinations
  • Familiarity with REST API concepts
  • Understanding of OAuth2 authentication
  • Basic knowledge of document embedding and vector search concepts

Architecture Overview:

Components:

  • Joule Agent: Your AI agent that interacts with users and utilizes document grounding
  • Document Grounding Tool: Enables semantic search across embedded document chunks
  • SAP AI Core: Provides embedding models and vector database capabilities for document grounding
  • Object Store (S3): Stores your documents and their metadata
  • SAP BTP Build Process Automation: is a comprehensive low-code/no-code platform enables Joule studio
  • SAP BTP: The underlying platform hosting all services

Setup Document Grounding tool in Joule Studio:

Step 1: Configure SAP BTP Destinations

1.1 Configure AI Core Destination:

a. In your subaccount, navigate to Connectivity → Destinations
b. Click New Destination
c. Configure with the following settings:

 

Field

Value

Notes

Name

ai-core

Use a descriptive name

Type

HTTP

-

URL

`<serviceurls>` 

From service key

Proxy Type

Internet

-

Authentication

'OAuth2ClientCredentials'-

Client ID

'<clientid>'

From service key

Client Secret

'<clientsecret>'From service key

Token Service URL

`<url>/oauth/token`

From service key
Token Service URL Type'Dedicated'-

Add the following additional property to the destination in order to appear it in Joule Studio:

 `sap-joule-studio-document-grounding-ai-core` `true`

!!! tip "Verify Destination"
 After creating the destination, use the Check Connection button to verify it's configured correctly.

1.2 Create Object Store (S3) Destination:

a. In your subaccount, navigate to Connectivity → Destinations
b. Click New Destination
c. Configure with the following settings:

 

Field

Value

Notes

Name

'object-store-s3'

Use a descriptive name

Type

HTTP

-

URL

'https://<host>'

Your S3 host URL

Proxy Type

Internet

-

Authentication

'OAuth2ClientCredentials'-

Client ID

'<access_key_id>'

Your S3 access key

Client Secret

'<secret_access_key>'Your S3 secret 

Token Service URL

'https://<host>'

Same as URL

Token Service URL Type'Dedicated'-

Add the following additional property to the destination in order to appear it in Joule Studio:

Key

Value

Description

sap-joule-studio-document-grounding-object-store

true

Identifies destination for document grounding

region

'<your-region>'

S3 region (e.g., `us-east-1`)

bucket

`<your-bucket>`

S3 bucket name

username

`<username>`

Username if required 

type'S3'

Specifies S3 as the object store type

!!! warning "Destination Name Consistency"
 Ensure the destination names are identical, if SAP Build and Joule are in separate subaccount.

Step 2: Create Document Grounding Resource Group in Joule Studio


Now that the SAP AI Core infrastructure is ready, configure document grounding in Joule Studio.

2.1 Access Document Grounding in Control Tower

a. Open SAP Build Lobby
b. Navigate to Control Tower
c. Select Document Grounding

2.2 Add a Resource Group

a. Click Add to create a new resource group
b. In the Add Resource Group dialog:

 Step i: Name
- Enter a descriptive name (e.g., `Product Documentation`)
- Click Next

 Step ii: Select AI Core Destination
- Destination Type: `HTTP`
- Authentication Type: `OAuth2ClientCredentials`
- Select your AI Core destination: `ai-core`
- Click Next

 Step iii: Select Object Store Destination
- Destination Type: `HTTP`
- Authentication Type: `OAuth2ClientCredentials`
- Select your object store destination: `object-store-s3`
- Click Next

c. Click Add to create the resource group

3.3 Monitor Resource Group Status

After creation, the resource group will go through a grounding process:

 

StatusDescriptionAction Required
PendingGrounding process is runningWait for completion
CompletedReady to use Can be added to agents
ErrorSomething went wrongCheck logs and retry


!!! info "Grounding Process"
The grounding process creates the necessary connections and validates that the AI Core resource group and collections are accessible. This may take a few minutes.

3.4 Manage Resource Groups

Once created, you can perform the following actions on resource groups:

  • Edit: Modify the resource group configuration
  • Refresh: Re-run the grounding process (useful if you've added new documents)
  • Delete: Remove the resource group


!!! note "Refresh vs New Documents"
 The Refresh action refers to re-running the grounding process in Joule Studio, not refreshing the AI Core resources. To add new documents, add them via AI Core API (Step 2.4), then refresh the resource group in Joule Studio.

Step 3: Create a Joule Agent

Now create a Joule agent that will use document grounding capabilities.

3.1 Create the Agent

a. In your Joule Studio project, click Create → Joule Agent
b. Name: Enter a descriptive name (e.g., `Product Support Agent`)
c. Description: Provide a meaningful description:
 ```
This agent assists customers with product-related questions by searching through
product documentation, manuals, and technical guides to provide accurate,
contextual answers.
 ```
d. Click Create

3.2 Configure Agent Expertise

The Expertise field defines the agent's role and focus area. Use role prompting to make the agent more specialized.

Example:
```
You are a knowledgeable product support specialist with expertise in our enterprise
software products. You provide accurate, helpful answers based on official product
documentation.
```
Best Practices:

  • Be short and clear (2-3 sentences)
  • Define a specific role or persona
  • Avoid being too general
  • Focus on the agent's domain expertise
3.3 Configure Agent Instructions

The Instructions field defines what tasks the agent should perform. Leave empty for conversational agents, or provide specific instructions for structured, repetitive tasks.

Example for Document Search Agent:
```
When a user asks a question:
1. Use the document search tool to find relevant information from the documentation
2. Read the retrieved content carefully
3. Provide a clear, accurate answer based on the documentation
4. If information is not found in the documents, clearly state that
5. Include relevant context and examples from the documentation when helpful
```

!!! warning "Conversational vs Structured"
- Conversational agents: Leave Instructions empty to allow adaptive responses
- Structured workflows: Provide detailed, step-by-step instructions

3.4 Add Additional Context

Use the Additional Context field to provide guidance on specific situations. All additional context should begin with `##`.

Example:

```markdown
##Tool Usage Guidelines
When using the document search tool, prefer specific search queries over broad ones.
For technical questions, prioritize searching for exact error codes or feature names.

## Response Formatting
When providing installation instructions, always format steps as numbered lists.
When referencing specific product versions, always include the version number.

## Handling Missing Information
If the documentation does not contain the answer, use the Final Answer tool to inform
the user and suggest alternative resources such as contacting support directly.
```

Prompting Guide: https://pages.github.tools.sap/ICN-AI-Agents/baf-documentation/documentation/general/prompting/

3.5 Configure Model Settings

a. LLM Provider: Choose your LLM provider (e.g., OpenAI, Azure OpenAI)
b. Base Model: Select the model for writing responses (e.g., `gpt-4o`)
c. Advanced Model: Select the model for planning and tool selection (e.g., `gpt-4o`)
d. Backup LLM: (Optional) Enable a backup LLM for resilience

!!! info "Model Postfixes"
- `-minimal`: Nearly no reasoning
- `-low`: Basic reasoning
- `-auto`: Model decides reasoning depth

3.6 Configure Agent Execution Steps
a. Maximum Number of Thinking Steps: Set to `10-15` for document search agents
  • Higher values allow more complex searches and reasoning
  • Lower values are faster but may miss complex queries

b. Pre-processing Steps: Enable (recommended)

  • Includes direct instructions, task decomposition, and planning
  • Helps break down complex questions

c. Post-processing Steps: Enable (recommended)

  •  Includes refinement and answer bundling
  •  Provides cleaner, more coherent responses
3.7 Configure Agent Output
a. Default Output Format:      
  • Text: Plain text (simple responses)
  • Markdown: Formatted text (recommended for document grounding)
  • JSON: Structured data
  • Data Type: Structured data with scheme

b. Allow Joule to interpret the output: Enable for conversational agents

  • Joule may rephrase or summarize responses
  • Disable for technical accuracy requirements

!!! success "Recommended Settings for Document Grounding"
- Output Format: Markdown (allows formatted responses with lists, headings, code blocks)
- Interpret Output: Enabled (for more natural conversations)

Step 4: Add Document Grounding Tool to Agent

Now integrate the document grounding tool with your Joule agent.

4.1 Add the Documents Tool

a. In your Joule agent configuration, scroll to the Tools section
b. Click Add Tool → Documents

4.2 Configure the Documents Tool

In the Add Tool dialog:
a. Name: Enter a descriptive name for the tool

Example:

```
Product Documentation Search
```

b. Description: Provide a clear description of what the tool searches

Example:

```
Searches through product documentation, user manuals, installation guides, and
technical specifications to find relevant information about our enterprise products.
Use this tool when answering questions about product features, installation,
configuration, troubleshooting, or best practices.
```

!!! tip "Description Best Practices"
- Be specific about what content is available
- Mention key topic areas covered
- Include when the tool should be used
- Use clear, actionable language

c. Resource Group: Select the resource group you created in Step 3

Only resource groups with status Completed are available. Example: `Product Documentation`

d. Click Add

5.3 Verify Tool Addition

After adding the tool, you should see it listed in the Tools section of your agent configuration:

  • Tool Name: `Product Documentation Search`
  • Tool Type: `Documents`
  • Resource Group: `Product Documentation`

You can edit or remove the tool at any time by clicking the corresponding icons.

Debugging & Issues:

  • Use the Trace Feature

Use Joule Studio's trace feature to debug and optimize:

a. Open the agent chat interface
b. Enable Trace or Debug Mode
c. Ask a question
d. Review: 

Which tools were called 

What search queries were used 

What documents were retrieved 

How the agent reasoned through the response 

  • Issue: Agent Doesn't Use Document Search Tool

Symptoms: Agent provides answers without searching documents

Possible Causes & Solutions:

a. Vague Tool Description: Make tool description more specific and actionable
```
Bad: "Search documents"
Good: "Searches product documentation including installation guides, user manuals,
and API references. Use this tool when answering product-related questions."
 ```

b. Missing Instructions: Add explicit instructions to use the tool
```
"When answering questions, use the 'Product Documentation Search' tool to find
relevant information before responding."
 ```

c. Too Many Tools: If agent has many tools, prioritize document search in instructions

For more troubleshooting refer this link: https://pages.github.tools.sap/ICN-AI-Agents/baf-documentation/documentation/ui-walkthrough/troubleshoot/

Reference Document: https://help.sap.com/docs/Joule_Studio/45f9d2b8914b4f0ba731570ff9a85313/4444cd1ce4cd471bbe127ea2e4735b40.html?locale=en-US

entry.title: Increasing Sales Performance: Why aren’t we talking about the cognitive load of sales travel?
entry.content:
Why aren’t we talking about the cognitive load of sales travel?

I travel a lot for work. In fact, I’m writing this from an aisle seat, drinking mediocre airline coffee. And while I have my packing system down to a science, the amount of mental effort that goes into booking travel is mind boggling. The plethora of small decisions – flight time, airline, hotel cost vs proximity to the office – add to the big ones, like which trips are worth taking because they drive business forward. It takes a lot of mental effort that could have been spent doing much more impactful work.

We spend so much time trying to crack the code of sales productivity. And we should. Non-selling activities still consume 2/3 of the average sales team’s time, according to McKinsey. What if we’re so focused on time savings that we’re overlooking the importance of protecting another precious resource: our sellers’ cognitive power? There’s an obvious productivity killer staring us right in the face:  constant micro-decisions unrelated to selling. These continual small choices might seem insignificant, but in reality when added up, come at a high cost in relation to the mental toll, taxing sales reps unnecessarily each day. .

Context Switching: The Silent Tax on Productivity

Today’s sales landscape requires continual context-switching. Our sellers jump from researching customers to trainings about new products to back-to-back customer calls. Modern CRMs like SAP Sales Cloud keep sellers focused and productive.; we’ve come a long way from the “swivel chair” concept. But to help our sellers be more productive and effective, decreasing context switching and the associated mental stain is critical.

My Product Marketing team just tried an exercise to illustrate the cognitive load of context switching:

  1. First, count aloud by 2s until you reach 20. Easy.
  2. Then, spell the word “strawberry” aloud. Also easy.
  3. Now, alternate one letter and one number: 2-S-4-T-6-R, and so forth. It’s embarrassingly difficult.

If selling is one activity, and booking travel is another, you can imagine how hard a rep’s brain is working during that switch, even if the rep isn't aware of it. But that productivity loss adds up in a big way. There’s a better way: making the task of booking travel as minimally taxing as possible thanks to a little help from our very smart friend:

Offloading Mental Effort, Ramping Up Sales

SAP thinks about these challenges just like you do, because we have sellers and sales leaders we care about, too. We’re integrating Booking Agent into SAP Sales Cloud, so now sellers can use Joule to book travel to visit their clients, directly from the screen where they’re working their deal.

Joule uses the details of the opportunity to create itinerary options for the salesperson to choose from. They can book right there by clicking a button, or they can tell Joule what they want to change. Joule handles all those micro-decisions, freeing up your seller so they can focus on selling.

When you challenge yourself to think differently about what you can do to make space for your sellers to be brilliant, you come to realize that eliminating unnecessary effort is what modern productivity looks like. And yeah, this saves time too.

(Booking Agent within SAP Sales Cloud Generally Available in Q2 2026)

entry.title: Reimagining Business Processes with Joule Agents - Answers to Your Top Questions
entry.content:
Recently, I had the opportunity to deliver a session titled “Reimagining Business Processes with Joule Agents,” via the AI live enablement session format Beyond Hype. 

After the session, I realized many of the questions raised, especially those related to architecture, integration, governance, and real-world adoption, weren’t just relevant to the live audience—they reflect what many organizations are currently trying to understand as they explore Joule agents more deeply. 

So instead of letting those questions stay in the room, I’m sharing them here. 

Below, I’ve compiled the answers to your top questions—organized into five themes to help you navigate the journey from understanding what Joule agents are, to building and running them, to scaling them with trust across your enterprise. 

The five themes: -  

  • The What — Understanding what Joule agents are and how they fit into your business  
  • The Where — Discovering where agents live in the SAP architecture and how to find them  
  • The How — Getting started, connecting systems, and accessing enterprise data 
  • Enterprise Readiness— Security, governance, reliability, and enterprise-wide adoption 
  • Commercial 

Let's get started!

 

 

The What: Understanding Joule Agents 

 1. How do Joule agents actually fit into my end-to-end business processes?

Joule agents are designed to integrate seamlessly into your business processes - not just as standalone helpers, but as active participants capable of orchestrating and executing complex workflows. 

How Joule agents fit into business processes: 

There are two key patterns: 

  1. Within a single business process: Many agents are designed to handle end-to-end tasks within a specific domain. For example, an accounting accruals agent can research historical data, assess current bank accounts, and propose which accruals should be posted - autonomously completing the entire workflow. 
  1. Across business processes: More sophisticated multi-agent systems can span multiple domains. Imagine a scenario where an agent processes a sales order, posts a goods issue, and then collaborates with a demand planning agent to inform supply chain and procurement decisions. While many agents today focus on specific processes, we're already building toward this cross-functional orchestration. 

 

Now, how do you interact with agents: 

Agents can be interacted with in three ways: 

  • Conversationally (In-Chat): Users can interact with agents via the Joule UI by asking questions or issuing requests.  Here's an example with a Performance Planning Agent. 

 

 

  • Embedded in applications (In-App): Agents can be integrated directly into business contexts. For instance, a customer service agent embedded in a case management tool can automatically analyze an open case, classify it, and suggest relevant knowledge articles - all without the user needing to leave their workflow.  Here's an example of it with CX (Customer Experience) Agents. 

 

 

  • System-Triggered (In-Background): This is where agents become truly proactive. System events rather than user actions can trigger them. For example, our trade classification agent continuously monitors evolving trade policies. When a new regulation affects how your products should be classified, the agent autonomously researches the update, proposes a reclassification, and seeks your approval, ensuring compliance without manual monitoring. 

 

 

The evolution of agents and integration with deterministic automations: 

Joule agents elevate traditional automation by handling the fluid, dynamic variables of your business. While deterministic automation excels at predictable, rule-based tasks, agents can adapt to changing contexts, make informed decisions, and orchestrate next steps intelligently.  

In future scenarios, you'll see hybrid models where agents manage unpredictable elements — like policy changes, customer sentiment, or market shifts — while standard automations handle the known, repeatable steps. Together, they create end-to-end processes that are not just reactive or even proactive, but truly adaptive. 

 

 

The Where: Discovering Agents & Architecture 

 

2. Is there a place to get a complete overview of available AI features and Joule agents? 

Of course there is! Do you know you can explore AI offerings in the AI Catalog on SAP Discovery Center? It shows all out-of-the-box AI Agents and Features by product area and business processes, spanning from Beta, Early Adopter Care, and generally available. Just click on a feature to see further resources, roadmap info, and pricing details. 

Now you might be wondering, what's the difference between an "AI Agent" and an "AI Feature"? 

AI Agent is a dynamic, agentic system designed for end-to-end task completion. It can autonomously execute multiple steps on behalf of the user- leveraging tools, collaborating with other agents, and utilizing existing business context or documents to accomplish complex goals.  

AI Feature, on the other hand, is typically a single-purpose capability. It takes one input and produces one output, such as a text summarization or a sentiment analysis. 

Learn in this video how to navigate the AI Feature catalog and estimate your costs. 

 

 

 

3. Where do Joule agents sit in the overall SAP architecture?   

Joule agents are the latest addition to SAP's AI capabilities, built on top of the SAP Business Technology Platform (BTP).  

Architecturally, they leverage several foundational BTP services and components: 

  • SAP AI Core: Provides the underlying AI infrastructure that powers agent execution and model orchestration. 
  • Joule: Serves a dual role: it acts as the conversational UI through which users interact with agents, and it also functions as an orchestrator that coordinates agent activities and workflows. 
  • Business Applications: Joule agents connect directly to data residing in SAP's cloud business applications (such as S/4HANA Cloud, SuccessFactors, Ariba, etc.), enabling them to operate within a real business context. 
  • SAP Business Data Cloud: For customers who have adopted it, Joule agents can also tap into this layer for broader access to harmonized and enriched enterprise data. 

In short, Joule agents don't exist in isolation - they sit at the top of SAP's AI stack on BTP, pulling together AI infrastructure, orchestration, conversational interfaces, and enterprise data to deliver intelligent, context-aware automation directly within your business landscape. Check out this blog for more details.

 

 

The How: Getting Started & Connecting 

 
 4. What do I need to get started with Joule agents — from technical prerequisites to activation steps?  

Getting started with Joule agents is straightforward once you understand the discovery and provisioning process. Here's a high-level walkthrough: 

Step 1: Discover Available Agents 

Start by exploring the AI Catalog in SAP Discovery Center. As explained in the first question, there you'll find the complete list of available agents, along with details about their capabilities, availability status (GA, Early Adopter Care, or Beta), and pricing. 

Step 2: Evaluate and Trial 

If you find an agent that fits your needs, check whether it's available for trial. The Discovery Center provides all the necessary information, including technical prerequisites such as BTP subaccount requirements, entitlements, connectivity settings, and configuration details. 

Step 3: Provision the Agent 

Once your organization has purchased the required entitlements, you can provision the agent directly through SAP for Me. We're also introducing enhanced capabilities that will guide you through the provisioning process, including the selection of prerequisite landscapes. BTP will handle the necessary connections in the background, as these are already configured within your BTP landscape- making activation smooth and seamless. 

What if I am still on-premise? Joule agents are cloud-native and run on SAP Business Technology Platform (BTP). On-premise customers can access these agents by connecting their systems to BTP using integration solutions like SAP Integration Suite or BTP connectivity services. This allows them to leverage the power of AI agents while keeping their core business systems on-premise. 

 
 

 5. How connected are Joule agents across SAP and third-party systems? How are these integrations technically enabled?  

SAP Joule agents are designed to connect to both SAP and third-party systems, including third-party agents. Here is how that connectivity works: 

Agent-to-Agent Communication 

Joule agents use an open standard agent-to-agent (A2A) protocol for communication. This enables both inbound and outbound interactions, meaning: 

  • SAP agents can call external agents 
  • External agents can call SAP agents 

What SAP has added on top of the open standard: 

SAP has introduced two additional primitives to make agent-to-agent communication enterprise-ready: 

  • System and User Context: In an enterprise landscape, an agent may execute a task for a specific organization, using a specific BTP sub-account and tenant. SAP has extended the A2A protocol to include this system context in communication. Additionally, user identity and authorization information are included, so that agents always operate within the user’s access rights on behalf of which they are running. Through an Agent Registry, these agents are made discoverable not only within SAP's ecosystem but also by third-party agents and systems. If you want to learn more about this, you can find information here.  
  • Agent Gateway: Once communication is established, SAP's Agent Gateway ensures that any agent interacting with an SAP agent is securely identified and authenticated. The gateway handles authentication mapping, authorization checks, and routing to the correct agent within the SAP ecosystem. This gateway is part of the Joule orchestrator, which serves as the single entry point for anyone looking to interact with SAP agents. 

Existing integration capabilities remain intact. 

While these primitives enable agent-to-agent communication, nothing changes for existing integration patterns. If you have a third-party or custom agent that needs to interact with a specific SAP system through an API, it continues to work as before. 

For SAP internal agent communication, if you want to reach any SAP internal agent through a third-party agent, the communication will always go through the Joule orchestrator. You can discover information about that SAP internal agent through a central Agent Registry. Once you have identified the specific agent, you can then communicate with it directly by making the call through its ORD endpoint. 

 

 

 6. How do Joule agents access and understand enterprise data?  

Joule agents access enterprise data through multiple mechanisms, depending on the landscape and data architecture in place: 

Data Access Paths 

  • Directly through business applications: Agents can access data from the business application on which they are built, leveraging the data layer it provides. 
  • Through SAP Business Data Cloud (BDC): BDC provides a unified semantic data layer that harmonizes data structures across different SAP applications. This makes data more consistent, better structured, and easier for AI and large language models to understand. BDC also offers a growing library of ready-to-use data products spanning business applications, giving agents consistent, cross-application data access through a single semantic layer. 

How agents understand business context 

  • For standard business applications, the business context is provided directly to the agent as part of its configuration. 
  • With SAP Knowledge Graph, agents also get a richer semantic business context layer, for a more robust, consistent connections between data and its semantic understanding across applications. 

Handling custom extensions 

This is an important topic for many customers. SAP is actively building capabilities that will enable agents to understand customer-specific custom extensions. These extensions will be surfaced to the agent so that it can account for any customizations made within a specific customer landscape. More details on this will be shared in the future as these capabilities mature. 

 

 

Enterprise Readiness 

 

 7. What security, governance, and compliance framework applies to Joule agents?  

Security, governance, and compliance are what make Joule agents enterprise-ready. Here is how SAP ensures this: 

Identity and Access Management 

SAP's own Identity Authentication Service (IAS) provides the foundation for security and governance across Joule agents. It ensures that every user and agent interaction is properly authenticated and authorized within your enterprise landscape. 

Enterprise-Ready Primitives 

As part of the agent-to-agent communication architecture, SAP has introduced two key primitives that reinforce security and governance: 

  • System and User Context: Every agent interaction carries system context (such as the specific BTP sub-account and tenant) along with user identity and authorization information. This ensures agents always operate within the user’s access rights on behalf of whom they are running. 
  • Agent Gateway: The Agent Gateway, part of the Joule orchestrator, ensures that any agent communicating with an SAP agent is securely identified, authenticated, and authorized before it can execute any task. It acts as the central checkpoint for all inbound agent communication. 

Compliance and Observability 

SAP adheres to its established enterprise compliance standards across Joule agents, ensuring that data access, processing, and agent interactions meet the governance requirements that organizations expect from their business software. Additionally, SAP is building observability directly into the agent architecture. This means every agent's actions and decisions are closely monitored and traceable, so for compliance or audit purposes, you can always see exactly what happened under the hood. 

 

 

Commercial 

 8. How are Joule agents licensed and priced — and how can organizations estimate their investment? 

Agent usage is measured in steps, and each step consumes requests from the user’s package or AI Units directly for consumption-based scenarios. 

For users within a Per-User-Per-Month package, Basic Agents consume 5 requests per step, Standard Agents consume 10 requests per step, and Advanced Agents consume 25 requests per step. These requests are drawn from the user’s fair use pool. 

For consumption-based scenarios such as customer-built agents in Joule Studio or agents not included in a package, Basic Agents consume 0.005 AI Units per step, Standard Agents consume 0.01 AI Units per step, and Advanced Agents consume 0.025 AI Units per step. 

Customer-built agents generally fall into the Basic category at present, with fewer steps required for simpler tasks. 

You can find more information here: SAP Business AI Commercial Model 

 

 

------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------
 
In case you are looking for further resources to learn how to build your own Joule agents, have a look at the curated list of enablement below: 

Here are your key resources to help you get started building your own Joule agents: 

  • How to Get Started with Joule Studio: Prerequisites, Commercials, and Instructions to begin using Joule Studio  
  • Joule Studio Goes Live: Answers to Your Top Questions covering Skills and the new Agent Builder features  
  • Create an Agent in Joule Studio: Step-by-step tutorial on how to build and test an agent in Joule Studio 
  • Best Practices for Building Agents: Key principles and practices for designing effective Joule Agents  
  • Discovery Center Mission: Get Hands-on experience in creating a Joule Agent with multiple skills and MCP 

 

 

Bookmark this blog as we share more information here.

entry.title: Unlock Intelligent Workflows with Introducing Joule
entry.content:
Joule is SAP’s new way to bring intelligence into daily work across SAP and non‑SAP systems. If you want a clear, fast introduction, start with Introducing Joule. It is a free, beginner‑friendly course that takes about one hour. You will also earn a completion badge when you finish.

In this course, you will see what Joule is and how it delivers connected, context‑aware experiences. You will learn how it supports real business outcomes like higher productivity, scalable automation, and enterprise‑grade trust.

You will meet the core parts of the Joule ecosystem. That includes Joule Skills, Joule Agents, and role‑based assistants. You will also see how different roles work with Joule, and how Joule adapts to each user’s context.

The course explains what you need in place to get started. It covers technical prerequisites and key commercial concepts. It also points you to trusted SAP resources, so you can stay current as Joule evolves.

Want to go further after you finish? Join optional live sessions like “Joule in ABAP,” “Getting Started With Joule Studio,” and “Building an Intelligent Joule Agent using Joule Studio in SAP Build.”

Start learning today. Build your foundation in Joule, and showcase your achievement with a course completion badge.

Introducing Joule

Summary

  • Understand what Joule is and how it powers connected workflows across SAP and non‑SAP systems.
  • Recognize Joule’s business value: higher productivity, scalable automation, and trusted, enterprise‑grade governance.
  • Explain the Joule ecosystem: Joule Skills, Joule Agents, and role‑based assistants.
  • See how roles interact with Joule through context‑aware experiences that adapt to user needs.
  • Identify prerequisites, commercial basics, and trusted SAP resources to adopt and stay current with Joule.

entry.title: 【保存版】Joule for Consultants (J4C) セルフサービス導入・完全ステップガイド
entry.content:
Joule for Consultants（以下 J4C）は、コンサルタントだけでなく、営業やプリセールスの生産性をも劇的に変える強力なツールです。

J4Cの導入を検討されている皆様についに朗報です！2026年に入り、待望の「SAP Storeでの直接購入」が可能になりました。

営業担当（AE）とのやり取りを待たず、セルフサービスでスピーディに導入できるようになった一方で、設定を一つ間違えると「契約したのに3ヶ月間も使えない」という深刻なトラブルに繋がるケースも……。

本記事では、最短ルートで導入するための具体的な手順と、実務担当者が絶対に踏んではいけない「落とし穴」を詳しく解説します。

まず、「ストア購入」vs「営業担当（AE）経由」どっちが向いている？

「セルフサービス（SAP Store）」と「営業担当（AE）経由」、どちらのルートで進めるべきか迷われるかもしれません。
結論から言うと、「まずは少人数で、今すぐ試したい」ならSAP Store、「全社導入でコストを最適化したい」ならAE経由がおすすめです。

具体的な違いを以下の表にまとめました。

項目SAP Store (セルフ)SAP AE (営業担当) 経由
手続きスピード最短当日（セルフ）数日〜数週間（調整による）
契約期間1年〜選択可能通常3年〜
価格交渉不可(定価販売)大規模導入の場合には割引の可能性あり
向いている人まず数名で試したい、急ぎ大規模導入、長期利用を予定

ステップ1：SAP Storeでの購入（AI Unitの契約）

まず、J4Cを利用するためのライセンス枠である SAP AI Units (SKU: 8019164) を契約します。

導入前に：J4Cの「数量計算」と「コスト」の考え方

J4Cはセルフサービスの購入では1ユーザーから、大きな初期投資なしで始められるのが最大の魅力です。

  • 消費の目安: 1ユーザーあたり 月間 35 AI Units

  • 最小購入単位: 100 AI Units（ ＝ 1ブロック ＝ リスト価格 約700ユーロ〜）

  • 10名で1年間使う場合の計算例: 10名 × 12ヶ月 × 35 Units ＝ 4,200 Units
    👉SAP Store等では 「42ブロック」 を購入することになります。

  • 柔軟な運用: 月単位で、利用ユーザーの変更や増減が可能です。

    • 例：4,200 Units購入し、最初の2ヶ月だけ30名で集中利用（2,100 Units消費）して有用性を判断する、といった使い方も可能です。

💡「10名以上」のススメ

J4Cは特定の個人に割り当てる「バイネーム形式」のライセンスです。アカウントの使い回し（シェア）は明確なライセンス違反となります。 組織として「本当に効果があるか？」を正しく判断するためには、コンサル、開発、PM、営業など複数のロールで活用を比較できるよう、まずは10名以上での検証を強く推奨しています。

購入の条件

以下の条件を満たす場合のみ、SAP Storeから直接購入可能です。

  • 過去に旧AI Unitを購入したことがない

  • 購入履歴が SKU 8019164（最新のAI Unit）のみ

  • （※TDD版のAI Unitの購入履歴については場合は全く問題ありません）

該当する場合は、SAP Storeで 「Buy as a customer」 を選択して購入してください。
これら以外の場合は、担当のAE（営業）を通した購入が必要です。

⚠️購入時の最重要チェックポイント：Customer ID (BPID)

購入プロセスで「どのCustomer IDに対して契約するか」を選択します。

これは「契約の主体」を決める作業です。 多くのパートナー企業様は「PartnerとしてのID」と「CustomerとしてのID」の二つを持っている可能性があり、どちらにライセンスを紐づけるかで、後の管理画面（SAP for Me）での見え方が変わります。 ここで間違ったIDを選んでしまうと、既存のS-Userでログインできず、有効化依頼（Case作成）ができないトラブルが発生します。

「解決に3ヶ月かかった」事例もあるため、必ず事前に社内で議論してください。

ステップ2：BTPアカウントの紐付け（基盤の準備）

購入プロセスの中で、J4Cをプロビジョニング（セットアップ）したい場所（SAP BTP Global Account）を指定します。

  • 既存アカウントがある場合: リストから適切な Global Account を選択します。部門専用の限定的なものではなく、中央管理用の共通アカウントが推奨されます。

    • ポイント: 将来的に Custom Joule Agent を独自開発する場合もAI Unitが必要です。SAP Build（開発ツール）を利用している既存のBTP Global Account があれば、そこを選択するのが最も効率的です。 

      ⚠️注意：BTP Global Account IDの確認

      BTPのGlobal Accountも会社の中で用途が違うものが複数ある可能性が高く、Joule で利用するBTP Global Accountを正しく指定する必要があります。ステップ1が「契約」なら、こちらは「設置場所」の指定です。
      前述の通り、J4Cのプロビジョニング先のBTP Global Accountを間違えると、ライセンスが届いているはずなのに「自分のBTP画面に表示されない」といったトラブルになり、解消に時間がかかります。

      必ず「J4CをデプロイしたいアカウントのID」を事前に確認してください。

       

  • 新規作成（Create New）の場合: 選択すると、SAPが無料で新しいBTPアカウントを発行します。数分〜数時間以内にログイン情報が届きます。

ステップ3：Discovery Centerでの有効化（テクニカルセットアップ）

購入完了後、実際の機能を有効化する作業が必要です。

  1. 公式ミッションの利用: SAP Discovery Center: Enable SAP Joule for Consultants にアクセスし、詳細な手順を確認してください。

  2. 主な設定作業:

    • サービスの有効化: BTPコントロールパネルから「Joule for Consultants」サービスを有効化。

    • ロールの割り当て: 利用者に Joule_Consultant_User 等のロールコレクションを割り当てます。

    • 認証設定: 必要に応じて SAP Cloud Identity Services (IAS) との連携を確認します。

最後に

「コンサル限定」から「全社的なSAPチャットボット」へ

J4Cは当初、技術者や機能コンサルタントを主なターゲットとしていました。しかし現在、活用シーンは劇的に広がっています。

  • プリセールス・営業: 顧客への提案資料のドラフト作成や、問い合わせへの回答アイデア出しに活用。

  • サポート部門: 複雑なSAP仕様の確認スピード向上。

あるパートナー企業では、「SAPビジネスに関わる全員」にJ4Cを配布したそうです。もはや技術者専用のツールではなく、「SAPに関するあらゆる疑問に答えてくれる社内専用AIアシスタント」として、営業からサポートまで幅広く活用するのがこれからのスタンダードになりそうです。

J4Cは「小さく始められ、月単位で柔軟に運用できる」非常に強力な投資対象です。2026年からは Development Fund (DF) の活用も可能になっています（※購入前のPBM相談が必須です！）。

組織としての導入メリットを正しく判断するためにも、まずは10ユーザー程度で、営業・コンサル・開発が一体となった検証からスタートしてみませんか？

詳細のJ4C検討ガイドについてはこちらをご覧ください。

entry.title: Master your AI Transformation – From AI Silos to the Agentic Enterprise with Patrick Kohler
entry.content:
This episode is a follow-up to an earlier conversation with Patrick Kohler on AI strategy and scaling. Since that recording, the landscape has shifted noticeably: Agentic AI has moved from a future concept to an active discussion in boardrooms and transformation programs. This episode picks up where the last one left off – with more concrete frameworks, updated observations from client projects, and a sharper focus on what separates organizations that scale from those that stay stuck in pilots.  (For a german speaking version listen in on Apple Podcasts · Spotify – or read the summary below).

Patrick Kohler, Head of AI Strategy and Advisory at SAP is focusing on how organizations can move from isolated AI projects to a scalable, strategically anchored Agentic AI architecture.

Why Agentic AI Is Not Just Another Hype

The fundamental difference between previous AI solutions and Agentic AI lies in the capacity to act. Classic Generative AI systems respond to requests – they deliver summaries, write emails, generate reports or find statistical patterns. Agents, by contrast, act actively: they can take non-deterministic steps, access tools and data, interact with other systems and people, and independently handle exceptions and deviations.

"Agents are proactive systems – they can overcome challenges autonomously, access tools and data, and interact across silos. That is exactly where most inefficiencies hide."

That is precisely where the real potential lies: in most enterprise processes, it is not the standard cases that consume time – it is the exceptions, the cross-departmental coordination, the situational adjustments. Agents can address these points. And in doing so, they transform an ERP system from a passive data-entry tool into an active nervous system that executes processes while keeping the human in the role of orchestrator. 

Key Numbers from the discussion

📊Numbers and Data 


Metric Figure
AI project failures rooted in people, process & org70%
AI project failures rooted in technology30%
Process improvement via Retrofit5–10%
Process redesign potential via Re-Engineering60–90%
RFQ process duration – before AI Usage20 days
RFQ process duration – after AI Usage1 day
Invoice intake automated via Document AI70%

These reflect patterns Patrick and his team observe consistently across client engagements. 

Why Nine Out of Ten AI Projects Fail – And What to Do About It

Only five percent of companies achieve AI value at scale. The reasons are well-documented: missing goal definitions and KPIs, a too strong technology focus without a clear business value, the sheer pace of innovation that makes it difficult to distinguish signal from noise – and insufficient enablement of the people involved.

Many organizations start with a use case that deploys the latest LLMs and platforms but addresses no concrete business problem. The result: pilot purgatory. Projects that never reach scale. Patrick's observation from the field aligns with what applied research consistently shows – the organizations that break through share a common set of behaviors, which he calls a "strategic AI playbook."

An overview on elements of an AI Playbook recently presented by Patrick on SAP Agentdays in Berlin. 

What Leading Companies Do Differently

  • First, they define a clear AI strategy from the outset, directly linked to the overall business strategy. AI strategy and business strategy are, in Patrick's view, no longer two separate topics.
  • Second, they do not manage isolated use cases, but run a dedicated AI program with top-down commitment from C-level, a sound governance structure, and a continuous roadmap.
  • Third, they rethink processes – not just automate them. This distinction – retrofit versus re-engineering – is one of the most consequential decisions an organization can make, and it is covered in detail below.
  • Fourth, they invest in their people. Enablement is not a nice-to-have but a prerequisite: the better the people are trained, the better the use cases become.

Retrofit vs. Re-Engineering: A Decision That Defines Your Return

This is one of the most underestimated distinctions in AI transformation. The choice between the two approaches determines not just the efficiency gain, but whether the investment delivers genuine transformation or merely incremental improvement.

Dimension Retrofit Re-Engineering
ApproachNew technology on existing processProcess redesigned around agents
EffortLower upfrontHigher upfront
Time to first valueFasterSlower initially
Efficiency gain5–10%60–90%
ScalabilityLimitedHigh
RiskLower short-termRequires change management
Best suited forStable, low-complexity processesHigh-frequency, exception-heavy processes
Human roleLargely unchangedShifts to orchestrator

Patrick's analogy captures the retrofit risk well: placing a Ferrari engine into a golf cart – you accelerate, but not safely, and the first accident is not far off. Re-engineering means asking, already at the process design stage: which steps should an agent handle? Where do I need a human in the loop? Where is my risk threshold? These questions are far more costly to answer retrospectively.

The Human-AI Interface: From Executor to Orchestrator

Patrick is clear on this point: humans are not being replaced – they are taking on a different, strategically more significant role. Moving away from data entry and passive execution, toward the orchestration of agents – comparable to a captain steering a ship while multiple systems operate in parallel.

"The human becomes the agent orchestrator – moving away from pure execution and data handling, toward truly strategic roles."

A thought Patrick introduces: every employee becomes, in a sense, a people manager – with agents as new team members that need to be onboarded, monitored, and replaced when their lifecycle is complete. This requires knowledge of what AI can and cannot do, clear intervention thresholds, and – critically – that these questions are built into process design from the start, not added as an afterthought.

The concept of the "Circle of Control" serves as a useful guiding principle here: the more autonomy an agent receives, the more governance is required.

Common Misconceptions Worth Addressing

These are patterns Patrick and his team encounter regularly in client collaborations. Each one is worth examining directly.

"AI is primarily a technology problem."
The data says otherwise. Seventy percent of AI project failures originate in people, processes, and organization. Technology accounts for only thirty percent. Most organizations, however, invest their attention in exactly the wrong proportion.

"A successful PoC means we are ready to scale."
The step from pilot to productive deployment is one of the most frequently underestimated transitions in AI programs. Governance, change management, and scalability need to be planned from the very beginning – not figured out after the pilot succeeds.

"Agents will replace my team."
Agents handle routine, repetitive, and exception-prone tasks. They free people to focus on judgment, domain expertise, and strategic decisions. The role changes; the human does not disappear.

"More AI tools equals more value."
Tool proliferation without strategic alignment produces exactly the silos the transformation is supposed to eliminate. Value comes from integration, not accumulation.

"Every process needs an agent."
Patrick is explicit: not every process benefits from an agent. Sometimes a simpler automation tool, an ML model, a digital workflow or even an unchanged process is the right answer. The starting point is always the business problem, not the technology. 

Flywheel Effects: When Scaling Actually Happens

A flywheel effect does not emerge automatically – it requires three preconditions: a continuous AI program with clear governance as the foundation; modular, reusable components – Patrick cites Document AI as an example deployed both in procurement (RFQ process reduced from 20 days to 1 day) and in accounts payable (70 percent of invoice intake automated); and the human as a multiplier.

When one business area experiences tangible value, demand and knowledge exchange spread organically to other areas. Communities, internal networks, and shared learning are not soft add-ons – they are structural components of a functioning flywheel.

The SAP Approach: AI Center of Excellence and Strategic Program

Patrick describes a three-step approach his team applies with customers: first, a baseline assessment using AI Discovery Workshops and Maturity Assessments. Then a focus on business value through process analysis and prioritized use cases. Finally, the translation into a structured roadmap – not a wish list, but a plan with clear technological and data dependencies.

"Think of AI as a team sport – build a strong partnership and jointly identify where Business AI makes sense and where it creates real value."

The AI Center of Excellence bundles these elements and helps organizations make the critical step from pilot to productive use. It is not simply a team of AI specialists – it is a structured organizational capability that connects business knowledge, process expertise, and AI technology in a continuous and consistent way.

Unpopular Opinion on AI Strategy

Patrick speaks of "agent washing" – analogous to the earlier "Gen AI washing": the tendency to label everything as an agent simply because the term currently dominates. His position: Agentic AI is a valuable tool, but not always the right one. Those who start from the business problem choose the tool that solves it most efficiently – and that does not necessarily have to be an agent.

Inspiring Statements

"70 percent of the reasons why AI projects fail lie in people, processes, and organization – not in technology."

"AI must be understood as a continuous journey, because the pace of innovation is significantly higher than with previous technologies."

"Investing millions of euros and placing the latest technology on top of an old process – you shouldn't be surprised if you don't unlock the full transformation potential of AI."

"The human becomes the agent orchestrator – moving away from pure execution and data handling, toward truly strategic roles."

"Think of AI as a team sport – build a strong partnership and jointly identify where Business AI makes sense and where it creates real value."

Practical Tips around AI Strategy

1. Align AI with Business Strategy
Do not treat AI as a separate IT topic. Link your AI ambition directly to your business objectives and define concrete KPIs that allow you to measure success – and to recognize when a use case is not delivering.

2. Set Up a Dedicated AI Program – Not a Project, Not a Silo
Instead of individual use cases, build a structured program with governance, C-level sponsorship, and a continuous pipeline. Only then does the flywheel effect emerge.

3. Start From the Business Problem, Not the Technology
Before deciding whether an agent, an ML model, or a classic automation tool is the right fit: define the pain point in the process first, then choose the appropriate tool.

4. Make a Conscious Choice Between Retrofit and Re-Engineering
For each process, assess whether optimization is sufficient or whether a fundamental redesign holds greater potential. Use ideation workshops with clear criteria: What data is available? Which KPIs should improve? Where is human in the loop necessary?

5. Plan the Step From Pilot to Production From the Start
The transition from POC to productive use frequently fails because it is planned too late. Build governance, scalability, and change management into the use case definition from the very beginning.

6. Try It Yourself – Don't Just Theorize
Patrick uses N8N and SAP Joule Studio to build agents himself. Hands-on experience helps develop realistic expectations and leads to better decision-making.

7. Use Notebook LM as a Learning Tool
Have studies and reports turned into podcasts and interact with the content directly – an efficient format for staying broadly informed in a fast-moving field. 

Summary

Anyone serious about AI transformation cannot avoid three things: a clear strategy with measurable goals, a structured program instead of isolated pilots, and the consistent involvement of people – not as a risk factor, but as the central shapers of the process. Agentic AI is not hype, but a tool with real potential – as long as it is approached from the business problem outward. Check out also the shownotes for further information and contact us if you want to reflect and craft your business AI strategy. 

Glossary from the podcast

Agentic AI – AI systems that act proactively, execute multi-step tasks autonomously, access tools and data, and handle exceptions without deterministic programming.

Agent Washing – The tendency to label any AI feature as an "agent" for marketing or relevance purposes, regardless of whether it actually exhibits autonomous, goal-directed behavior.

AI Center of Excellence (AI CoE) – A structured organizational capability that connects business knowledge, process expertise, and AI technology to govern, scale, and continuously improve AI adoption across an enterprise.

Circle of Control – A governance principle stating that as agent autonomy increases, the level of human oversight and governance required must increase proportionally.

Flywheel Effect – A self-reinforcing cycle in which early AI successes generate organizational momentum, knowledge sharing, and demand that accelerate further adoption and value creation. The more data, the more

Human in the Loop – A design principle where human judgment, approval, or oversight is built into an AI-driven process at defined intervention points, particularly for high-risk or high-stakes decisions.

Pilot Purgatory – The state in which AI use cases remain permanently in the proof-of-concept phase, never reaching productive deployment or generating measurable business value at scale.

Re-Engineering – The fundamental redesign of a business process with agents in mind from the start, enabling efficiency gains of 60–90% by rethinking roles, steps, and human-agent collaboration.

Retrofit – The application of new AI technology to an existing, largely unchanged process, typically yielding incremental improvements of 5–10%.

Strategic AI Playbook – A set of organizational behaviors consistently observed in companies that successfully scale AI: clear strategy, dedicated program governance, process redesign, and systematic people enablement. 

Resources and Shownotes from the podcast

LinkedIn – Patrick Kohler

SAP Business AI – Product Page

SAP BTP AI Best Practice Library

Previous episode with Patrick: Strategy and Scaling of Business AI in Practice

Blog: AI Agent Transformation with Philipp Sütterlin

Blog: AI Agents at SAP with Jochen Schneider

Andrew Ng Newsletter "The Batch" – deeplearning.ai

Book recommendation: Reshuffle – Who Wins When AI Restacks the Knowledge Economy

EducationNewscast Podcast – LinkedIn Newsletter text summary

Episode on Apple Podcasts Episode on Spotify

SAP Joule Studio – Integrated agent development within the SAP ecosystem

entry.title: SAP Joule Copilot responses issue for BP or Sales process in S/4HANA Private Cloud Edition
entry.content:
Hi Experts/Gurus,

I have recently enabled Joule Copilot specifically joule base within S/4HANA Private Cloud edition 2023 version. It works fine for informational & navigational scenarios. However, when i am testing it for transactional capabilities like creating business partner or creating sales order with reference. It doesn't work as expected and it's hard to figure out what's the root cause as all standard roles & backend OData are active.

I am attaching screenshots as of now but i can give more details going forward if someone will engage on this post.
In sales order creation process, it asked for sales order type which i gave but then it stopped, no response at all but then i tried to ask simple information like when this sales order was created but it throws this error that its taking long time to get details from backend.
In BP process, it looks like there is no app for creating BP but i do have access of Manage Business Partner master data. So, hard to understand what went wrong here as well.
Joule SAP S/4HANA Cloud Private Edition 
@

entry.title: SAP Joule – Common Configuration Issues
entry.content:
Are you interested in activating Joule and integrating it seamlessly into your system—but running into errors along the way? In my role as solution architect for our SAP S/4HANA Customer Care and Regional Implementation Group I’ve worked with some of our first Joule customers and would like to share how you can avoid and resolve errors during the activation.

This blog highlights the most common SAP Joule issues encountered during provisioning, booster execution, and system integration.

You’ll also find clear, step-by-step guidance to help you quickly troubleshoot and succeed with your activation.
Please also refer to our SAP Build Work Zone, standard edition and Joule Health Check blog to test your activation and configuration is working correctly before using Joule https://community.sap.com/t5/technology-blog-posts-by-sap/sap-build-work-zone-standard-edition-and-joule-health-checks/ba-p/14348321

We will look at the following topics:

  1. SAP Business Technology Platform (BTP) subaccount / provisioning issues
  2. Joule booster problems
  3. User Experience (UX) / icon appearance issues
  4. SAP Cloud Identity Services - Identity Provisioning service (IPS) and Identity Authentication service (IAS) issues
  5. Restrictions/ constraints
  6. Monitoring and troubleshooting
  7. References
  8. How to contact SAP Support

1. SAP BTP subaccount / provisioning issues

Issue

Solution/Available fix

You have a license for an SAP product that supports Joule integration (such as SAP SuccessFactors, SAP S/4HANA Cloud Private Edition, etc.) and after creating a subaccount the Joule service is not available in the subaccount entitlement.

Check SAP Help:Data Centers Supported by Joule

The data centre is supported but the entitlements are not available in your global account.

Contact your SAP Account Manager/Executive. 

Refer to SAP Note 3639572 - Enabling Joule with SAP S/4HANA Cloud Private Edition

SAP Help:Joule prerequisites

The menu entry "System Landscape" is not visible in the Global account of your SAP BTP Cockpit.

You are not an administrator of the Global account.

SAP Note 3456718 - Menu "System Landscape" is not visible in the Global account of SAP BTP Cockpit

SAP Note 2669325 - How to add a user as a SAP Business Technology Platform Global Account Administrator

SAP systems are not visible in SAP BTP under the system landscape.

 

 

Before adding your system, make sure it's not already auto-discovered and listed automatically on the System Landscape ---> Systems page.

Refer to SAP Help: BTP Registering SAP System
SAP Note 3661817 - SAP systems are missing in System Landscape of the BTP Cockpit

 2. Joule Activation/Issues during the execution of the Joule booster

While running the booster configuration for the Joule Service, the prerequisites check failed for the Identity Authentication Tenant.

 An error message is displayed: "Your global account does not have an Identity Authentication service tenant."

 

Ensure that the IAS tenant is visible under the global account and configure trust between the Identity Authentication Service (IAS) and the Joule subaccount. 

SAP Note 3675105 - Joule Setup Error: "Your global account does not have an identity authentication service tenant"

 

 

 

The Joule booster is being run, and you encounter the following error:

*********************************
Could not find SAP Cloud Identity system with host <IAS tenant>. 
Report an incident in the BC-CP-MP component 

The SAP Cloud Identity Services tenant isn't connected to the correct SAP BTP Global Account, or it's linked to the wrong customer ID.
How to Fix it:
a. Make sure your IAS tenant is linked to the correct Global Account.
Check the SAP Help:View Assigned Tenants and Admins.

b. If you have multiple IAS tenants , use the IAS tenant that matches the Global Account's customer ID.
Important:Data syncing between systems usually takes up to one week.Wait for this to complete before running the booster again so that the SAP IAS system appears in all the accounts.

You can manually trigger the sync via the “Refresh” button.
SAP Note 3620409 - Could not find SAP Cloud Identity system with host: Joule booster error

Enabling Joule leads to the following error: 
"Prerequisites for the formation type "Integration with Joule" are not fulfilled.
No SAP Cloud identity service system is showing up under“System landscape” --->System

 

A new SAP BTP Global Account will take some time to synchronize all existing customer systems, but you can manually trigger the sync via the "Refresh" button.
This should ensure that your SAP Cloud Identity Services system shows up. Afterwards you can proceed with the Joule configuration.

 

The SAP Build Work Zone system does not appear in the dropdown list during the setup process (Joule Booster - Step 5 Set Up Integrations).

 

 

The SAP Build Work Zone service was not subscribed to under the relevant subaccount.
An instance of this service is not enough for the integration; therefore, the system will not appear under the options.

Add the SAP Build Work Zone subscription in the Joule subaccount. Refer to
SAP Note 3710864 - SAP Build Work Zone system not visible in dropdown during setup

Unable to find the SAP S/4HANA Cloud Private Edition option in Step 3 - "Select Integration" stage dropdown during activation by means of the Joule Booster.

In global account Systems, the SAP S/4HANA Cloud Private Edition system is already auto-discovered.

Ensure that it is visible in Joule Booster Step 04  "Select Capabilities", select "S/4HANA Private Cloud" and proceed to complete the Joule booster.

Refer to SAP Note 3710921 - S/4HANA Private Cloud option is not listed in Step 3 - "Select Integration" in Joule Booster for detailed information.

Error message: "The subscription of subaccount <xxxxxx-xxxx-xxxx-xxxx-xxxxxxxxx> to application das-application and plan foundation, failed with state: SUBSCRIBE_FAILED."

Observe the error messages indicating SaaS Subscription failed and instructions to open a support case or unsubscribe and retry.

 

Refer to SAP Note 3689452 - Entity Saas Subscription failed after running Joule Booster for detailed instructions.

If the issue persists, raise a support incident and choose component CA-JOULE-PRV-ONB for further investigation.

 

 

 3. UX/ Icon appearance issue

Joule refused to frame 'https://xxx.xxx.cloud.sap/' because an ancestor violates the following Content Security Policy directive: "frame-ancestors”.                             

 

 

 

 

Enable 3rd party cookies of your browser.Add the trusted domain in SAP IAS and SAP BTP
a. Add trusted domain in SAP IAS in the SAP Cloud Identity Services tenant.
Go to Applications & Resources -->Tenant Settings --> Trusted Domains--> Click the + (Add) button
Type the URL of the trusted host into the field.

b. Add the trusted domain in SAP BTP.
In SAP BTP cockpit, navigate to your specific subaccount ---> Security ---> Settings.
Click the + (Add) button.
Enter the required hostname, for example: https://<your-s4-or-sfsf-url>.com

The Joule icon at the top app bar in the Fiori Launchpad is not properly displayed.

The issue is caused using an unsupported SAPUI5 version in the SAP S/4HANA Cloud Private Edition system. 

 

Upgrade the SAPUI5 Component version of SAP S/4HANA to be equal to or higher than the minimum supported version.

SAP Notes:
2346367 - How to upgrade the SAPUI5 library in ABAP - SAP for Me
3641945 - Joule icon displayed wrongly in Fiori launchpad of SAP S/4HANA Cloud Private Edition 
 

After integrating Joule with SAP Solutions, the user is unable to launch Joule by clicking the diamond icon,  it fails to load or respond.

 

 

Allow third-party cookies.

SAP Note 3643889 - User unable to launch the Joule Icon after integrating with SAP Solutions

The Joule icon is not appearing for a specific user despite that the correct role-based permissions have been assigned in SAP SuccessFactors.

The issue is most likely caused by security policies of the network provider, which may be blocking certain URLs that are required for the icon to appear.
The affected user does not have the General User Permission --->Access to Joule

Refer to SAP Note 3685621 - Joule button not appearing on SuccessFactors

After clicking on the Joule icon in SAP SuccessFactors, it tries to load for a few minutes and subsequently the user gets a blank or greyed-out screen.

Refer to SAP Note 3544482 - Getting Error / White /Blank / IAS login Screen When Trying to Launch Joule in SuccessFactors

4. SAP IPS/IAS issues

The SAP IAS source system read job is failing.

 

Keep the source system name the same as in the content channel that had been created in the standard SAP Build Work Zone.

Joule was successfully integrated with the SAP product. However, clicking the Joule icon results in a blank white or SAP IAS login screen. Single sign-on is not working.

 

Check the SAP IAS domain. In the SAP BTP Joule subaccount, go to Trust Configuration and select the Custom Identity Provider for Applications.

Change the field "Domain" to the one used in the SAP product.

Refer to SAP Note 3635949 - Blank white / IAS login screen appears when clicking on Joule icon from the solutions where Joule was integrated into

5. Restrictions /Constraints
SAP Joule operates within specific constraints.
SAP notes:
3698807 - How to Remove or Hide the Joule Icon in SAP S/4HANA Cloud
3711505 - Restrictions: Multiple different Work Zone editions integration with Joule
Before starting the Joule project, please refer to the document below. All constraints applicable to Joule are listed here.
SAP Help:Joule constraints

6. Monitoring and troubleshooting
You may experience issues that would have to be reported in SAP incidents. In such cases, please collect information, which will help the support team to investigate and resolve the problem quickly.
SAP Note 3666014 - Information required for Jouletroubleshooting
SAP Help:Monitoring and Troubleshooting

7. How to report issues
You can report an incident or error through SAP for Me. Ensure that you provide a detailed description of the issue including the steps to reproduce it. Attach any relevant screenshots, logs, or error messages to help with the investigation. Please use the appropriate components listed below when creating your incident.

General support component for core Joule services issue and functionality

CA_JOULE

Joule integration with SAP S/4HANA Cloud Private Edition

CA-FLP-EXT-PCE-JOULE

SaaS Subscription error after running Joule Booster

CA-JOULE-PRV-ONB

8. Frequently asked questions

  1. Are there options available to customize the theme of Joule so it aligns with our branding guidelines and specific colour palette?

The theme tab is only available when Joule is used in a standalone (single page) mode. For now, Joule automatically adapts to the theme of the embedding application.  For example, if the user will changes the theme in the SAP Work Zone settings, Joule will automatically adopt it. The user cannot choose a different theme for the Joule client.
SAP Note 3646989 - Is it possible to adjust theming for Joule?

  1. Is it possible to remove the Joule icon for selected users?

When Joule is enabled through the Joule booster, it is activated for all users in both SAP S/4HANA Public Cloud and SAP S/4HANA Private Cloud systems. At present, there is no supported option to restrict or disable Joule for specific users or user groups.
SAP Note 3698807 - How to Remove or Hide the Joule Icon in SAP S/4HANA Cloud

Summary
In this blog, we reviewed common configuration and activation issues related to Joule including challenges with SAP Business Technology Platform provisioning, Joule booster activation, UX visibility, and integration with SAP Cloud Identity Services (IPS/IAS), along with key restrictions, monitoring tips, and reference resources.

In addition, we discussed current system restrictions and limitations, monitoring options, log analysis techniques, and practical troubleshooting steps to help identify and resolve issues efficiently.

By following the guidance and best practices outlined above, administrators can streamline Joule activation, minimize configuration errors, and ensure a smoother user experience. If problems persist, contact SAP Support with detailed information such as error messages, screenshots, tenant details, and relevant logs to accelerate root cause analysis and resolution.

Brought to you by the SAP S/4HANA Customer Care and RIG team

entry.title: Title: From Hype to Hands-On: Level Up Your AI Skills This March
entry.content:
In the age of AI, the skills we build today will define how we work tomorrow. That’s why we’ve launched a monthly blog series that spotlights the latest AI learning assets and upcoming learning opportunities.

Building on my first blog, where I introduced you to our AI live sessions Beyond Hype (which is all about getting productive with SAP Business AI), I wanted to address some of your most asked questions. But before I do that, I’m excited to share that the sessions are now ungated and no longer require a SAP Learning Hub subscription!

  1. What methodology should customers follow to modernize with RISE with SAP, and what environment prerequisites must customers meet to enable the AI toolchain?

    The recommended approach for modernization is the RISE with SAP methodology—a single, standardized framework that guides customers end-to-end across their transformation journey. Built on SAP Activate and enriched with proven best practices, it provides a structured path from discovery to run, while embedding clean core principles and quality gates to ensure consistency, governance, and partner alignment throughout. 

    This methodology is complemented by an integrated toolchain enabled by SAP Business AI. With the RISE with SAP package, key tools such as SAP Signavio, SAP LeanIX, and SAP Cloud ALM are already included, so that customers can start without additional licenses for the standard AI-powered setup. However, the bundled offering covers foundational capabilities. Advanced or specialized features in Signavio and LeanIX may require additional licenses.

    Beyond the commercial bundling, SAP also supports customers operationally through onboarding and the Transformation Preparation Service (TPS), helping set up and configure the toolchain within the customer’s landscape from day one.



  2. How are changes in solutions like SAP Signavio, SAP LeanIX, and SAP Cloud ALM automatically synchronized across the toolchain?

    SAP Signavio is the leading system for process modelling; it’s where processes are designed, planned, and maintained. These models are then pushed to SAP Cloud ALM, where they are used to run and manage projects. If changes to a process are required, they are made in SAP Signavio first, and the updated models are then re-synchronized with SAP Cloud ALM. This ensures a clear “single source of truth” in Signavio, with Cloud ALM always working on the latest version.

  3. What do I need to get started with Joule agents — from technical prerequisites to activation steps?

    Getting started with Joule agents is straightforward once you understand the discovery and provisioning process. Here's a high-level walkthrough:

    Step 1: Discover Available Agents
